teaching.explanation: A variable is a named storage location that holds a value. Every value has a type — int, float, string, boolean — which tells the computer how much memory to allocate and what operations are legal. For example, you can add two ints (5 + 3 = 8) but you cannot meaningfully add a string and an int without converting first. Declaring a variable like `int age = 25` tells the computer: reserve 4 bytes of memory, call it 'age', and put the number 25 there.
teaching.analogy: Think of a variable as a labeled box. The label is the variable name, the size of the box is the data type, and the contents are the value. A box labeled 'age' that fits only whole numbers (integers) cannot hold a decimal like 25.7 — you'd need a bigger box labeled 'float'.

validation_question.prompt: What does a variable's data type determine?
options: How much memory is used and what operations are valid
The order in which code runs
validation_question.correct: How much memory is used and what operations are valid

quiz.q: Which data type would you use to store the value 3.14?
In `int count = 0`, what is 'count'?
Why can't you store the text 'hello' in an int variable?
options: Because int variables are too slow
Because the data type doesn't match — int only holds whole numbers

teaching.explanation: An array is a contiguous block of memory that stores multiple values of the same type, accessed by index. If you have 10 student scores, instead of 10 separate variables you use one array: `int scores[10]`. Indexes start at 0, so `scores[0]` is the first element and `scores[9]` is the last. Because each element is the same size and they sit side by side in memory, the computer can jump to any element in constant time — just calculate: start address + (index × element size).
teaching.analogy: An array is like a row of numbered mailboxes in an apartment building. Box 0, Box 1, Box 2 … all the same size, all in a line. You can instantly go to mailbox 7 without checking 0 through 6 first, because you know exactly where it is.

validation_question.prompt: If an array has 5 elements, what is the index of the last element?

quiz.q: Why can arrays access any element in O(1) time?
options: Because they use a hash function
Because elements are stored contiguously and the address is calculated directly
Because they cache the most recent access

quiz.q: What happens if you try to access index 10 in an array of size 5?
All elements in a standard array must be:

teaching.explanation: A loop repeats a block of code while a condition is true. The two most common are `for` (when you know exactly how many iterations) and `while` (when you repeat until something changes). A for loop over an array — `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)` — is extremely common: i starts at 0, runs while i is less than the array length, and increases by 1 each time. The loop body executes n times total. Loops are what make algorithms work on inputs of any size.
teaching.analogy: A loop is like a factory assembly line. The conveyor belt keeps moving items forward and performing the same action on each one until there are no more items. The 'while condition is true' part is the sensor that stops the belt when the bin is empty.

validation_question.prompt: How many times does this loop run? `for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)`

quiz.q: What is an infinite loop?
options: A loop that runs exactly 10 times
A loop whose condition never becomes false
A loop that skips every other element

quiz.q: In `for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++)`, what does `i` represent?
Which loop type is best when you don't know in advance how many times to repeat?

teaching.explanation: Linear search scans an array from the first element to the last, comparing each one to the target until it finds a match or exhausts the array. It is the simplest search algorithm and requires no preconditions — the array does not need to be sorted. The worst case (target not found or at the very end) checks every element: O(n) time. It's practical for small arrays or unsorted data, but slow on large collections.
teaching.analogy: Linear search is like looking for a specific name in a stack of unalphabetized index cards — you flip through each card one by one from top to bottom. You might get lucky and find it on the first card, or you might have to check all 1,000 before giving up.

validation_question.prompt: What is the worst-case time complexity of linear search on an array of n elements?

quiz.q: Linear search requires the array to be sorted.
On average, linear search checks how many elements before finding the target?
When is linear search a reasonable choice?
options: When the array has a million elements
When the array is small or unsorted
When you need the fastest possible search
When the array is already sorted

teaching.explanation: A sorted array has all its elements arranged in order — typically ascending. This one property unlocks much faster searching algorithms. When you look at the middle element and compare it to your target, you can immediately discard half the array: if your target is smaller, it must be in the left half; if larger, the right half. Without sorting, you cannot safely skip any elements. Maintaining a sorted array costs O(n) for insertions (you must shift elements) but the search speedup is often worth it.
teaching.analogy: Think of a sorted array as a dictionary vs. a random word list. In a dictionary, if you open to the middle and see 'Monkey' but you're looking for 'Apple', you know to go left. In a random list, you have no such shortcut.

validation_question.prompt: If you're looking for the number 42 in a sorted array and the middle element is 75, where must 42 be?
options: 42 is definitely not in the array

quiz.q: What is the key advantage of a sorted array over an unsorted one?
options: Searching can be done much more efficiently

quiz.q: Why does sorting enable you to eliminate half the array in one comparison?
options: Because order lets you infer where elements can and cannot be
Because sorted arrays use hash maps internally
Because the computer reads sorted arrays faster

quiz.q: What is the downside of keeping an array sorted?
options: You can no longer search it
Insertions require shifting elements — O(n) cost
Sorted arrays cannot store negative numbers

teaching.explanation: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space. Start with pointers at low=0 and high=n-1, compute mid=(low+high)/2. If arr[mid] equals the target, done. If target < arr[mid], search the left half (high = mid-1). If target > arr[mid], search the right half (low = mid+1). Each step eliminates half the remaining elements. After k steps, roughly n/2^k elements remain — it takes at most log₂(n) steps. For 1,000,000 elements that's only ~20 comparisons vs. 500,000 on average for linear search.
teaching.analogy: Binary search is exactly how you find a word in a physical dictionary. You open to the middle, see if your word is alphabetically before or after, flip to the middle of the remaining half, and repeat. You never re-examine the discarded half.

validation_question.prompt: What is the time complexity of binary search?

quiz.q: What is the absolute prerequisite for binary search to work correctly?
options: The array must have an even number of elements
The target must exist in the array
The array must have no duplicates

quiz.q: In binary search on an array of 1024 elements, at most how many comparisons are needed?
What happens to the search space after each comparison in binary search?
options: It is cut roughly in half
It is cut to one quarter

teaching.explanation: Divide and conquer is a problem-solving strategy with three steps: (1) Divide — split the problem into smaller subproblems of the same type. (2) Conquer — solve each subproblem, usually recursively, until they are trivial. (3) Combine — merge the solutions to form the answer to the original problem. The key insight is that many large problems become simple when small enough. This strategy underlies binary search (divide the array), merge sort (divide then merge), and many other efficient algorithms.
teaching.analogy: Imagine organizing a 64-team tournament bracket. Instead of ranking all 64 teams at once, you split them into two groups of 32, find the best in each group, then play those two winners against each other. Each group does the same split — divide, conquer, combine.

validation_question.prompt: Which of the following best describes the 'divide' step in divide and conquer?
options: Break the problem into smaller subproblems of the same type
validation_question.correct: Break the problem into smaller subproblems of the same type

quiz.q: What are the three steps of divide and conquer?
Binary search is an example of divide and conquer because it:
options: Splits the search space in half and works on one subproblem

quiz.q: The 'combine' step is most important in which algorithm?

teaching.explanation: Bubble sort repeatedly steps through the array, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. Each full pass bubbles the largest unsorted element to its correct position. After n-1 passes, the array is sorted. Selection sort instead finds the minimum element in the unsorted portion and swaps it to the front, growing the sorted portion by one each pass. Both algorithms run in O(n²) time — quadratic — because for each of n passes they do O(n) work. They are simple to understand but impractical for large data.
teaching.analogy: Bubble sort is like a line of people arranging themselves by height: you walk the line comparing pairs side-by-side and swapping if needed, then repeat. Selection sort is like picking the shortest person out of the remaining crowd and placing them at the front of the sorted line, one at a time.

validation_question.prompt: What is the time complexity of bubble sort and selection sort?

quiz.q: In bubble sort, what does each full pass through the array guarantee?
options: The largest unsorted element moves to its correct position
The smallest element moves to the front

quiz.q: Selection sort works by:
options: Finding the minimum in the unsorted part and placing it at the sorted boundary
Splitting the array in half recursively
Inserting each element into a sorted sub-array

quiz.q: Why are bubble sort and selection sort impractical for large arrays?
options: They require too much extra memory
Their O(n²) runtime grows too fast as n increases
They can only sort numbers, not strings
They require the array to already be partially sorted

teaching.explanation: Recursion is when a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. Every recursive function must have: (1) a base case — a condition where it stops and returns directly, and (2) a recursive case — where it calls itself with a simpler input. For example, factorial(n) = n × factorial(n-1), with factorial(0) = 1 as the base case. Each call is pushed onto the call stack. When the base case is reached, the stack unwinds and results combine. Without a base case, recursion runs forever — a stack overflow.
teaching.analogy: Recursion is like standing between two mirrors facing each other — each reflection contains a smaller version of the same scene. The 'base case' is the tiny reflection in the distance that's too small to show the next one.

validation_question.prompt: What happens if a recursive function has no base case?
options: It calls itself forever until the program crashes (stack overflow)
It automatically finds a stopping point
validation_question.correct: It calls itself forever until the program crashes (stack overflow)

quiz.q: What are the two required parts of a correctly written recursive function?
options: A base case and a recursive case
A sort step and a search step

quiz.q: Each recursive call is added to the:
What does factorial(3) evaluate to using recursion? (factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1), factorial(0) = 1)

teaching.explanation: Every recursive algorithm has two kinds of behavior. The base case is the simplest possible input — one where you can answer directly without recursing (e.g., an empty array has no maximum). The recursive case is everything else: you make the problem one step simpler, call yourself, and use the result. Good recursive design means clearly identifying both. A flawed base case (wrong condition, missing return) is the #1 cause of bugs in recursive code. Think: 'What is the smallest input I can answer instantly? That's my base case.'
teaching.analogy: Recursion is like a set of Russian nesting dolls. The recursive case is every doll that opens to reveal a smaller one. The base case is the innermost doll — it doesn't open. You can't build the set without knowing exactly which doll is the smallest.

validation_question.prompt: For a recursive function summing all elements in an array, what should the base case be?
options: When the array has exactly two elements
When the array is empty — return 0
validation_question.correct: When the array is empty — return 0

quiz.q: Which statement correctly describes a base case?
options: It is where the function makes its recursive call
It is the condition under which the function returns a direct answer without recursing
It is the function's return type
It is only needed for sorting algorithms

quiz.q: What is the recursive case responsible for?
options: Reducing the problem and calling the function on the smaller input
Checking if the input is null

quiz.q: If you write a recursive function with the right recursive case but a wrong base case, what most likely happens?
options: The function works correctly most of the time
The function either crashes or gives wrong answers

teaching.explanation: Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm. Step 1 (Divide): split the array in half. Step 2 (Conquer): recursively sort each half. Step 3 (Combine): merge the two sorted halves into one sorted array. The merge step walks two pointers across the two halves, always picking the smaller element — this takes O(n). The recursion has O(log n) levels. Total: O(n log n), which is dramatically better than bubble sort's O(n²) on large inputs. Merge sort is stable (preserves order of equal elements) and predictable — it always runs in O(n log n).
teaching.analogy: Imagine sorting a hand of playing cards by repeatedly splitting the deck into halves, sorting each half, and then merging them back together. Two people can sort two halves simultaneously, then you interleave the sorted halves by always picking the smaller top card from either pile.

validation_question.prompt: What is the time complexity of merge sort?

quiz.q: In merge sort, what does the 'merge' step do?
options: Combines two sorted halves into one sorted array

quiz.q: Why is merge sort faster than bubble sort for large arrays?
options: Merge sort's O(n log n) grows much slower than bubble sort's O(n²)
Merge sort only works on already-sorted arrays
Merge sort never performs any comparisons

quiz.q: What is the base case of the recursive merge sort function?
options: An array with 0 or 1 elements (already sorted)
An array whose first element is the smallest
An array with an even number of elements

teaching.explanation: A stack is a data structure that follows Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) order. You can only add (push) or remove (pop) elements from the top. The most recently pushed item is the first to be popped. Stacks appear everywhere: the call stack in recursion, undo history in text editors, the browser's back button, and expression evaluation in calculators. Common operations: push(x) — add to top, pop() — remove from top, peek() — see top without removing. All three are O(1).
teaching.analogy: A stack is exactly like a stack of plates. You add plates to the top and take them from the top. You cannot remove the plate at the bottom without first removing all the plates above it. The last plate you added is the first one you'll use.

validation_question.prompt: If you push 1, 2, 3 onto a stack in that order and then pop once, what is returned?

quiz.q: What does LIFO stand for?
Which real-world feature is implemented using a stack?
options: A print queue (jobs print in order submitted)
A browser's back button (last visited page first)
A to-do list (tasks completed in any order)
A search result list (sorted by relevance)

quiz.q: What does the 'peek' operation do on a stack?
options: Adds a new element on top
Returns the top element without removing it
Searches the stack for a value

teaching.explanation: Big-O notation describes how an algorithm's runtime scales as the input size n grows. Common complexities from fastest to slowest: O(1) constant (doesn't depend on n), O(log n) logarithmic (binary search — halves the problem each step), O(n) linear (one loop), O(n log n) linearithmic (merge sort), O(n²) quadratic (nested loops — bubble sort), O(2ⁿ) exponential (recursive subset enumeration). Big-O ignores constant factors and lower-order terms — O(3n + 100) is just O(n). It describes the worst-case growth rate, telling you how the algorithm behaves at scale.
teaching.analogy: Big-O is like asking 'if my city doubles in population, how much longer does my commute take?' O(1) means no change (you have a helicopter). O(n) means twice as long (proportional to population). O(n²) means four times as long — catastrophically worse. It's about the shape of growth, not the exact time.

validation_question.prompt: An algorithm with two nested loops, each running n times, has what time complexity?

quiz.q: What does O(1) mean?
options: The algorithm runs in exactly 1 second
The runtime is constant regardless of input size
The algorithm only works on inputs of size 1

quiz.q: Why does Big-O ignore constant factors like '3' in O(3n)?
options: Constants don't change the growth rate as n approaches infinity
Big-O is only used for sorting algorithms

quiz.q: Which ordering correctly ranks complexities from best to worst?
options: O(n²) < O(n log n) < O(n) < O(log n) < O(1)
O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²)
O(log n) < O(1) < O(n) < O(n²) < O(n log n)
O(n) < O(1) < O(log n) < O(n log n) < O(n²)

teaching.explanation: A tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has a value and zero or more child nodes. Every tree has a single root node at the top. Nodes with no children are called leaves. The relationship is strictly parent-to-child with no cycles — this distinguishes a tree from a general graph. A binary tree is a special case where every node has at most two children (left and right). Trees model hierarchies naturally: file systems, HTML DOM, organizational charts, and decision trees. The depth of a node is how many edges from the root; the height of the tree is the maximum depth.
teaching.analogy: A tree is like a family tree. The great-grandparent is the root. Each person's children branch below them. Leaves are people who have no children yet. You can trace any path from the root down to any leaf without ever going backward.

validation_question.prompt: What is the root of a tree?
options: The single top-level node with no parent
The node with the largest value
validation_question.correct: The single top-level node with no parent

quiz.q: What is a leaf node in a tree?
options: A node with exactly one child

quiz.q: In a binary tree, each node has at most:
Which of these is NOT an example of a tree structure?
options: A file system with folders and files
An HTML document with nested tags
A doubly linked list with prev and next pointers

teaching.explanation: Tree traversal visits every node exactly once in a defined order. Three classic orders for binary trees: (1) In-order (left → root → right): for a binary search tree, this visits nodes in sorted ascending order. (2) Pre-order (root → left → right): useful for copying or serializing a tree. (3) Post-order (left → right → root): useful for deleting nodes or evaluating expression trees — process children before parents. All three are naturally implemented with recursion. Each traversal is O(n) because every node is visited once.
teaching.analogy: Think of traversal like reading a book outline. Pre-order is like reading the chapter title, then all sub-sections — you see the heading before the details. In-order is like reading page by page left to right. Post-order is like summarizing every sub-section before writing the chapter summary.

validation_question.prompt: For a binary search tree, which traversal visits nodes in sorted ascending order?

quiz.q: In pre-order traversal, when is the root node visited?
What is the time complexity of any standard tree traversal on a tree with n nodes?
Post-order traversal processes children before the parent. This makes it useful for:
options: Printing a tree in sorted order
Deleting a tree (free children before freeing parent)
